metadata.summary: Efficiently computes derivatives of NumPy code.

documentation: # Autograd [![Checks status][checks-badge]][checks-url] [![Tests status][tests-badge]][tests-url] [![Publish status][publish-badge]][publish-url] [![asv][asv-badge]](#) [publish-badge]: https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/actions/workflows/publish.yml/badge.svg [checks-badge]: https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/actions/workflows/check.yml/badge.svg [tests-badge]: https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/actions/workflows/test.yml/badge.svg [asv-badge]: http://img.shields.io/badge/benchmarked%20by-asv-green.svg?style=flat [publish-url]: https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/actions/workflows/publish.yml [checks-url]: https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/actions/workflows/check.yml [tests-url]: https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/actions/workflows/test.yml Autograd can automatically differentiate native Python and Numpy code. It can handle a large subset of Python's features, including loops, ifs, recursion and closures, and it can even take derivatives of derivatives of derivatives. It supports reverse-mode differentiation (a.k.a. backpropagation), which means it can efficiently take gradients of scalar-valued functions with respect to array-valued arguments, as well as forward-mode differentiation, and the two can be composed arbitrarily. The main intended application of Autograd is gradient-based optimization. For more information, check out the [tutorial](docs/tutorial.md) and the [examples directory](examples/). Example use: ```python >>> import autograd.numpy as np # Thinly-wrapped numpy >>> from autograd import grad # The only autograd function you may ever need >>> >>> def tanh(x): # Define a function ... y = np.exp(-2.0 * x) ... return (1.0 - y) / (1.0 + y) ... >>> grad_tanh = grad(tanh) # Obtain its gradient function >>> grad_tanh(1.0) # Evaluate the gradient at x = 1.0 0.41997434161402603 >>> (tanh(1.0001) - tanh(0.9999)) / 0.0002 # Compare to finite differences 0.41997434264973155 ``` We can continue to differentiate as many times as we like, and use numpy's vectorization of scalar-valued functions across many different input values: ```python >>> from autograd import elementwise_grad as egrad # for functions that vectorize over inputs >>> import matplotlib.pyplot as plt >>> x = np.linspace(-7, 7, 200) >>> plt.plot(x, tanh(x), ... x, egrad(tanh)(x), # first derivative ... x, egrad(egrad(tanh))(x), # second derivative ... x, egrad(egrad(egrad(tanh)))(x), # third derivative ... x, egrad(egrad(egrad(egrad(tanh))))(x), # fourth derivative ... x, egrad(egrad(egrad(egrad(egrad(tanh)))))(x), # fifth derivative ... x, egrad(egrad(egrad(egrad(egrad(egrad(tanh))))))(x)) # sixth derivative >>> plt.show() ``` See the [tanh example file](examples/tanh.py) for the code. ## Documentation You can find a tutorial [here.](docs/tutorial.md) ## End-to-end examples * [Simple neural net](examples/neural_net.py) * [Convolutional neural net](examples/convnet.py) * [Recurrent neural net](examples/rnn.py) * [LSTM](examples/lstm.py) * [Neural Turing Machine](https://github.com/DoctorTeeth/diffmem/blob/512aadeefd6dbafc1bdd253a64b6be192a435dc3/ntm/ntm.py) * [Backpropagating through a fluid simulation](examples/fluidsim/fluidsim.py) * [Variational inference in Bayesian neural network](examples/bayesian_neural_net.py) * [Gaussian process regression](examples/gaussian_process.py) * [Sampyl, a pure Python MCMC package with HMC and NUTS](https://github.com/mcleonard/sampyl) ## How to install Install Autograd using Pip: ```shell pip install autograd ``` Some features require SciPy, which you can install separately or as an optional dependency along with Autograd: ```shell pip install "autograd[scipy]" ``` ## Authors and maintainers Autograd was written by [Dougal Maclaurin](https://dougalmaclaurin.com), [David Duvenaud](https://www.cs.toronto.edu/~duvenaud/), [Matt Johnson](http://people.csail.mit.edu/mattjj/), [Jamie Townsend](https://github.com/j-towns) and many other contributors. The package is currently being maintained by [Agriya Khetarpal](https://github.com/agriyakhetarpal), [Fabian Joswig](https://github.com/fjosw) and [Jamie Townsend](https://github.com/j-towns). Please feel free to submit any bugs or feature requests. We'd also love to hear about your experiences with Autograd in general. Drop us an email! We want to thank Jasper Snoek and the rest of the HIPS group (led by Prof. Ryan P. Adams) for helpful contributions and advice; Barak Pearlmutter for foundational work on automatic differentiation and for guidance on our implementation; and Analog Devices Inc. (Lyric Labs) and Samsung Advanced Institute of Technology for their generous support.

functions.name: autograd.tracer.primitive
documentation:
Wraps a function so that its gradient can be specified and its invocation
can be recorded. For examples, see the docs.

variables.name: autograd.tracer.isbox
documentation: almost 3X faster than isinstance(x, Box)

modules.name: autograd.scipy.stats.t
documentation: Gradients of the univariate t distribution.

modules.name: autograd.scipy.stats.norm
documentation: Gradients of the normal distribution.

functions.name: autograd.scipy.stats.multivariate_normal.generalized_outer_product
documentation: With thanks to Eric Bresch.
Some formulas are from
"An extended collection of matrix derivative results
for forward and reverse mode algorithmic differentiation"
by Mike Giles
https://people.maths.ox.ac.uk/gilesm/files/NA-08-01.pdf

variables.name: autograd.scipy.special.psi
documentation: psi(x) is just polygamma(0, x)

variables.name: autograd.scipy.special.digamma
documentation: digamma is another name for psi.

functions.name: autograd.numpy.numpy_wrapper.concatenate_args
documentation: ----- Special treatment of list-input functions -----

classes.name: autograd.numpy.numpy_wrapper.r_class
documentation: ----- Enable functions called using [] ----

functions.name: autograd.core.def_linear
documentation: Flags that a function is linear wrt all args

functions.name: autograd.numpy.linalg.T
documentation: Some formulas are from
"An extended collection of matrix derivative results
for forward and reverse mode algorithmic differentiation"
by Mike Giles
https://people.maths.ox.ac.uk/gilesm/files/NA-08-01.pdf
transpose by swapping last two dimensions

functions.name: autograd.numpy.linalg._matrix_diag
documentation: batched diagonal, similar to matrix_diag in tensorflow

functions.name: autograd.numpy.linalg.add2d
documentation: add two dimensions to the end of x

functions.name: autograd.numpy.linalg.grad_eigh
documentation: Gradient for eigenvalues and vectors of a symmetric matrix.

functions.name: autograd.numpy.linalg.grad_eig
documentation: Gradient of a general square (complex valued) matrix
https://arxiv.org/pdf/1701.00392.pdf Eq(4.77)
Note the formula from Sec3.1 in https://people.maths.ox.ac.uk/gilesm/files/NA-08-01.pdf is incomplete

functions.name: autograd.numpy.linalg.grad_svd
documentation: https://j-towns.github.io/papers/svd-derivative.pdf
https://arxiv.org/abs/1909.02659

functions.name: autograd.numpy.fft.fft_grad
documentation: TODO: make fft gradient work for a repeated axis,
e.g. by replacing fftn with repeated calls to 1d fft along each axis

functions.name: autograd.numpy.fft.check_no_repeated_axes
documentation: # TODO: could be made less stringent, to fail only when repeated axis has different values of s

functions.name: autograd.numpy.fft.make_rfft_factors
documentation:
make the compression factors and compute the normalization
for irfft and rfft.

modules.name: autograd.misc.optimizers
documentation:
Some standard gradient-based stochastic optimizers.

These are just standard routines that don't make any use of autograd,
though you could take gradients of these functions too if you want
to do meta-optimization.

These routines can optimize functions whose inputs are structured
objects, such as dicts of numpy arrays.

functions.name: autograd.misc.optimizers.unflatten_optimizer
documentation:
Takes an optimizer that operates on flat 1D numpy arrays and returns a
wrapped version that handles trees of nested containers (lists/tuples/dicts)
with arrays/scalars at the leaves.

functions.name: autograd.misc.optimizers.sgd
documentation:
Stochastic gradient descent with momentum.
grad() must have signature grad(x, i), where i is the iteration number.

functions.name: autograd.misc.optimizers.rmsprop
documentation: Root mean squared prop: See Adagrad paper for details.

functions.name: autograd.misc.optimizers.adam
documentation:
Adam as described in http://arxiv.org/pdf/1412.6980.pdf.
It's basically RMSprop with momentum and some correction terms.

modules.name: autograd.misc.flatten
documentation:
Handy functions for flattening nested containers containing numpy
arrays. The main purpose is to make examples and optimizers simpler.

functions.name: autograd.misc.flatten.flatten
documentation:
Flattens any nesting of tuples, lists, or dicts, with numpy arrays or
scalars inside. Returns 1D numpy array and an unflatten function.
Doesn't preserve mixed numeric types (e.g. floats and ints). Assumes dict
keys are sortable.

functions.name: autograd.numpy.numpy_jvps.broadcast
documentation: TODO(mattjj): can we call np.broadcast_to or a related function instead?

modules.name: autograd.differential_operators
documentation: Convenience functions built on top of `make_vjp`.

functions.name: autograd.differential_operators.grad
documentation:
Returns a function which computes the gradient of `fun` with respect to
positional argument number `argnum`. The returned function takes the same
arguments as `fun`, but returns the gradient instead. The function `fun`
should be scalar-valued. The gradient has the same type as the argument.

functions.name: autograd.differential_operators.elementwise_grad
documentation:
Returns a function that computes the sum of each column of the Jacobian of
`fun`, in one pass. If the Jacobian is diagonal, then this is the diagonal
of the Jacobian.

functions.name: autograd.differential_operators.jacobian
documentation:
Returns a function which computes the Jacobian of `fun` with respect to
positional argument number `argnum`, which must be a scalar or array. Unlike
`grad` it is not restricted to scalar-output functions, but also it cannot
take derivatives with respect to some argument types (like lists or dicts).
If the input to `fun` has shape (in1, in2, ...) and the output has shape
(out1, out2, ...) then the Jacobian has shape (out1, out2, ..., in1, in2, ...).

functions.name: autograd.differential_operators.grad_named
documentation:
Takes gradients with respect to a named argument.
Doesn't work on *args or **kwargs.

functions.name: autograd.differential_operators.make_hvp
documentation:
Builds a function for evaluating the Hessian-vector product at a point,
which may be useful when evaluating many Hessian-vector products at the same
point while caching the results of the forward pass.

functions.name: autograd.differential_operators.hessian_tensor_product
documentation:
Builds a function that returns the exact Hessian-tensor product.
The returned function has arguments (*args, tensor, **kwargs), and for
vectors takes roughly 4x as long to evaluate as the original function.

functions.name: autograd.differential_operators.tensor_jacobian_product
documentation:
Builds a function that returns the exact tensor-Jacobian product, that
is the Jacobian matrix left-multiplied by tensor. The returned function
has arguments (*args, tensor, **kwargs).

functions.name: autograd.differential_operators.make_jvp_reversemode
documentation:
Builds a function for evaluating the Jacobian-vector product at a
point. Roughly 1.5x more FLOPs than forward-mode, plus memory requirements
that scale with the number of primitives applied in the evaluation of f, as
well as other overheads. See j-towns.github.io/2017/06/12/A-new-trick.html.

functions.name: autograd.differential_operators.make_ggnvp
documentation:
Builds a function for evaluating generalized-Gauss-Newton-vector products
at a point. Slightly more expensive than mixed-mode.
TODO(mattjj): update this function using make_jvp and const_graph

functions.name: autograd.differential_operators.value_and_grad
documentation:
Returns a function that returns both value and gradient. Suitable for use
in scipy.optimize

functions.name: autograd.differential_operators.grad_and_aux
documentation:
Builds a function that returns the gradient of the first output and the
(unmodified) second output of a function that returns two outputs.

functions.name: autograd.differential_operators.checkpoint
documentation:
Returns a checkpointed version of `fun`, where intermediate values
computed during the forward pass of `fun` are discarded and then recomputed
for the backward pass. Useful to save memory, effectively trading off time
and memory. See e.g. arxiv.org/abs/1604.06174.

modules.name: autograd.extend
documentation: Exposes API for extending autograd

functions.name: autograd.numpy.numpy_vjps.grad_rollaxis
documentation: ----- Trickier grads -----

functions.name: autograd.numpy.numpy_vjps.repeat_to_match_shape
documentation:
Returns the array g repeated along axis to fit vector space vs.
Also returns the number of repetitions of the array.

functions.name: autograd.numpy.numpy_vjps.array_from_args_gradmaker
documentation: ----- extra functions used internally  -----
